title:Compact Disc+記憶座標:
When an entire generation is forced to forget rather than choosing to, how can memory be preserved? Can friendship become a gentle yet persistent form of resistance? The camera follows the director and a close friend as they return to the black hole of memory; through low-key and restrained imagery, unspeakable emotions are left to ferment in the dark. More than just a documentary, this is a memorandum of youth written for one another—an exercise in collective remembering. Amidst gloomy times, through love and companionship, it pieces together a bewildered yet authentic portrait of a generation of Hong Kongers.

**2026 CPH:DOX Copenhagen International Documentary Film Festival 【Asian Premiere】**

**Map of Traces**
CHAN Hau-chun｜Hong Kong｜2025｜DCP｜Colour, B&W｜29 min

A reflection on the landscapes of Hong Kong and the subtle traces left behind by its people. Within the memories of those who have left, stayed, or drifted, the film searches for intimate moments to connect one another. Blending media memories of Hong Kong’s major thoroughfares, correspondence with friends in prison, and street art, it explores the precise yet fluid moments between "presence" and "absence."

Unfolding as a tender letter, the film traces Hong Kong through its landscapes and lingering marks. Within memories of those who left, stayed, or drifted, it searches for intimate moments that quietly connect lives, attuned to the city’s subtle rhythms of change and stillness.

title:JSA共同警戒區 4K數位修復版:
The masterpiece that established director Park Chan-wook’s international reputation and became the highest-grossing South Korean film of its year, *Joint Security Area* returns for a limited 26th-anniversary 4K restored theatrical release. Through a cinematic lens that interweaves suspense and human nature, the film exposes both cruel realities and heartwarming humanity.

In the Joint Security Area (JSA) on the border between North and South Korea, two North Korean soldiers are shot dead one night under mysterious circumstances. Sophie, a Swiss-Korean female officer from a neutral nation, is dispatched to investigate the baffling case. As she peels back the layers of the investigation, she uncovers a shocking truth tied to a secret friendship that transcends national borders and historical animosity. Even as nations are pitted against each other by political hatred, a bond of brotherhood manages to cross the invisible 38th parallel. Through a seemingly casual yet precious photograph, the film conveys a profound yearning for peaceful coexistence.
title:Videotage X Forum Lenteng：鱗光映盪:
"Knock, knock, knock." A knock at the door in the dead of night shatters the silence. At first, only the woman hears it; a few days later, the same sound returns, and this time, her husband hears it too. Who is outside? Who is disturbing them from the shadows? Through interviews with a couple who have just moved into a new home, and using everyday objects like chalk and photographs, this thriller experience is reconstructed frame by frame. Private anxieties seep into the Indonesian cultural context, weaving a lingering atmosphere of horror.

2003 SGIFF Singapore International Film Festival
2005 Yokohama Triennale

**Diaspora (Generasi Sekian)**
Ivonne KANI｜Indonesia｜2015｜DCP
title:世外:
★ 2025 Golden Horse Award for Best Animated Feature

After death and before reincarnation, departed souls arrive at a mystical realm known as "The Beyond." Here, Spirit Guardians spend their days guiding souls toward their next lives. This cycle continues until a young Spirit Guardian, Little Ghost, encounters "Little Sister," a soul who refuses to move on. Through her, the once-emotionless Little Ghost begins to see The Beyond in a completely different light.

As he attempts to lead her toward rebirth, Little Ghost uncovers the regrets of her past life. Overwhelmed by fury, Little Sister triggers an irreversible curse. If she is completely consumed by her hatred, both The Beyond and the mortal realm will be reduced to ash along with her.

Little Ghost convinces the Celestial Maiden to grant Little Sister a final chance at redemption. The Maiden dispatches the formidable warrior Heitian to accompany them on a perilous quest spanning a thousand years. Despite the constant dangers, Little Ghost repeatedly risks his own life to save her, hoping only that Little Sister can let go of her past pain, forgive herself, and finally step lightly onto the path of reincarnation.
title:亂:
1986 Blue Ribbon Awards: Best Film
1986 Mainichi Film Awards: Best Film, Best Director, Best Supporting Actor
1986 Academy Awards: Winner for Best Costume Design; Nominated for Best Director, Best Cinematography, and Best Art Direction

Lord Hidetora Ichimonji, a Sengoku-era warlord who once dominated the battlefield with peerless authority, finds himself weary and aging at seventy. He decides to abdicate and divide his castles among his three sons. However, his eldest son immediately turns against him, subjecting him to humiliation and forcing him into exile. His second son also plots betrayal, treating his father with cold indifference. Only the third son—who was banished for his blunt honesty—secretly sends protection and warmly welcomes his exiled father. Witnessing the bloody fratricide of his lineage and tormented by the sins of his past, the once-mighty Hidetora descends into madness, silenced by the cruel tides of fate.

A thematic counterpart to *Kagemusha* that reaches even greater artistic heights, the film masterfully weaves Shakespeare’s tragedy *King Lear* into the Japanese Warring States period. Tatsuya Nakadai’s profound acting creates an intense emotional resonance, serving as the cornerstone of the film’s grand epic spirit. Although the production reused costumes and props from *Kagemusha*, it still cost $10 million to stage its massive battle sequences—a record-breaking budget in Japanese cinema at the time. The result is Akira Kurosawa’s ultimate masterpiece, the culmination of a lifetime of cinematic mastery.
title:二百三高地:
1981 Blue Ribbon Awards: Best Actor & Best Supporting Actor
1981 Kinema Junpo: Readers' Choice Award
1981 Japan Academy Film Prize: Winner of Best Actor, Cinematography, and Lighting; Nominated for Best Picture, Director, Screenplay, Best Supporting Actress, Art Direction, Score, and Sound.

The Meiji Restoration propelled Japan into the ranks of the world's leading powers. Its expansionist ambitions toward Northeast China and Korea provoked the Russian Empire, a traditional European power, bringing the two nations to the brink of war. To secure control over Port Arthur, the Russian army constructed what was considered the most formidable fortress in military history on the nearby "203 Hill," prepared to deliver a crushing blow to the Japanese. General Maresuke Nogi, fiercely loyal to the Emperor, led the well-equipped Third Army, determined to seize this strategic stronghold at any cost.

In a departure from his role in *Kagemusha* released that same year, Tatsuya Nakadai portrays the "War God" Nogi with restrained, commanding authority. His nuanced performance captures the crushing emotional toll of duty, a legendary portrayal that moved audiences to tears. Toei spared no expense in creating magnificent sets, enlisting Toshio Masuda—renowned for his war epics—to direct. The film’s contrast between brutal, bloody combat and the pure essence of the Bushido spirit sparked intense debate and ignited a trend for large-scale war spectacles. Following the normalization of diplomatic relations between Japan and China in 1972, Japanese films were banned from import in Taiwan; this film was screened only once during the inaugural Golden Horse International Film Exhibition in 1980 and has not been released there since.
title:人生海海:
★ Winner of the Best Supporting Actress at the 62nd Golden Horse Awards.

After years of working in Taiwan, Ah Yao returns to his hometown to handle his father’s funeral alongside his younger siblings. However, the proceedings are disrupted when the Religious Affairs Bureau intervenes, attempting to seize the father’s remains. In the face of this sudden conflict, the three siblings react in starkly different ways: the eldest brother, Ah Yao, chooses to yield; the younger brother, Ah Cai, opts for direct confrontation; and their sister, Shui Yun, struggles to maintain the sanctity of the ritual and order.

As the funeral progresses, long-buried family secrets begin to surface—tales of smuggling, migration, illness, and the lifelong struggle to obtain legal identification. Caught between memory and reality, the three are forced to re-examine their father and confront their own sense of belonging in this land.
title:人間的條件 第一部 純愛篇・第二部 激怒篇:
Top 10 Japanese Films (5th Place) & Best Actress, Kinema Junpo 1960
San Giorgio Prize & Premio Pasinetti, Venice Film Festival 1960

In 1943, Kaji, a man driven by humanist ideals, travels with his wife Michiko to the Tiger Ridge mine in Manchuria to serve as a wartime labor supervisor. He attempts to reform the colonial system from within and improve the treatment of prisoners of war, but he is constantly marginalized by bureaucrats and pressured by the military, all while failing to win the trust of the mistreated Chinese laborers. As the machinery of war relentlessly strips away his choices, Kaji finds himself increasingly isolated. How can he protect the dignity of those around him? And how can he preserve his own conscience as a human being?

Following *Black River*, a young Tatsuya Nakadai collaborates with director Masaki Kobayashi for the second time. His righteous and restrained performance captures Kaji’s impossible dilemma, marking a major breakthrough in his acting career. This realist masterpiece, adapted from Junpei Gomikawa’s novel of the same name, stands as a pinnacle of Japanese anti-war cinema. Cinematographer Yoshio Miyajima’s static shots and black-and-white widescreen photography establish a steady, solemn visual style, grounding this cinematic epic in a cold and piercing gaze at reality.
title:仲代達矢，為演員而活:
In 1975, Tatsuya Nakadai and his creative partner and wife, Kyoko Miyazaki, co-founded the acting school Mumeijuku. Nakadai personally oversees talent selection and intensive training, maintaining a rigorous schedule from 6 a.m. to 8 p.m. When performing, he suppresses his own presence, allowing only the character to collide with his scene partner. Mumeijuku does more than just train actors; it cultivates "human beings" capable of standing on their own in the world, producing a steady stream of talent for 50 years. Yet, beneath his roles as a teacher and a performer, Nakadai’s true self is that of a humble actor who hand-copies his lines with a calligraphy brush and pastes them on his bedroom walls for constant memorization. 

Director Hidetaka Inozuka spent over a year following Nakadai, capturing the training process at Mumeijuku and its theatrical performances. Interspersed with interviews with Nakadai and his disciples, the film documents a side of him rarely seen by the public. At the time of filming, Nakadai was in his 80s, yet he remained steadfast on his beloved stage, battling a fading memory and declining physical strength. He continues to embrace the "beginner’s mind" of his early days as a "nobody," fulfilling his life’s mission—to live for the craft of acting—and passing this conviction on to the next generation.
title:切腹:
1962 Kinema Junpo Top 10 Japanese Films (3rd Place)
1963 Cannes Film Festival Special Jury Prize
1963 Blue Ribbon Awards for Best Actor & Best Screenplay

In 1630 (the seventh year of the Kanei era), the centralized rule of the third Tokugawa Shogun, Iemitsu, weakened the power of the daimyo houses, leaving once-proud samurai to wander as masterless ronin. During this time, many ronin began extorting money from daimyo estates by threatening to commit ritual suicide (seppuku) on their grounds—a nuisance that plagued many, including the prestigious Ii clan. One day, an elderly ronin named Tsugumo Hanshiro arrives at the Ii estate, requesting a place to commit seppuku. The clan's retainers decide to call his bluff, intending to force him to follow through with the act. However, as the old man slowly recounts the story of his journey, his true intentions are gradually revealed.

Following *The Human Condition*, Tatsuya Nakadai and Masaki Kobayashi collaborate once again. Based on Yasuhiko Takiguchi's novel *Ibun Ronin-ki* and written by legendary screenwriter Shinobu Hashimoto, the film employs a non-linear narrative to build a masterful atmosphere of suspense. Captured by cinematographer Yoshio Miyajima, known for his grand visual scale, the film depicts a tragic and brutal tale of revenge. In an era and nation that glorified the code of Bushido, this film offers a bold and scathing critique—a spirit that Tatsuya Nakadai has frequently praised, citing this as one of his favorite works.
title:刺蠻王:
The swordsman Jin Shange originally intended to serve his country by joining the ranks of Marshal Zhang Zhe. However, he was framed for the murder of the Prime Minister's son and sentenced to exile at the frontier. During his transport, the escort was ambushed by Jin soldiers; in the chaos, Jin Shange broke free from his shackles, defeated the enemy, and escaped.

Meanwhile, Marshal Zhang Zhe was killed by the Jin commander, Wanyan. The Marshal's daughter, Enzhu, managed to flee to Lu Village with the help of Wang Feng. Seeking vengeance, they began recruiting heroes to their cause. Jin Shange happened to pass by their martial arts tournament, where his peerless skills impressed Wang Feng, who invited him to join the resistance against the Jin invaders. Jin Shange readily agreed, but on one condition: he must marry Enzhu. To avenge her father, Enzhu consented, provided that Jin Shange first return with Wanyan’s head.

Jin Shange initially presents himself as a decadent, roguish, and even lecherous figure, subverting the traditional "chivalrous hero" archetype of wuxia cinema. As the group unites to fight the Jin, this unconventional warrior gradually redeems his image, ultimately making a stylish, solitary exit reminiscent of a classic Western hero.
title:喀布爾，信仰之間:
Amidst the echoes of prayer and rifles slung over shoulders, faith and weaponry define the everyday. Two devout Afghan brothers live within this world: the elder patrols his post, dreaming of martyrdom while burdened by mundane worries; the younger, still a child, spends his days herding, playing, and reciting scripture, looking up to his brother with an innocence written across his face. Eschewing judgment for an open lens, the film offers a microscopic look at the coming-of-age of males under Taliban theocracy. What outsiders perceive as indoctrination is, to them, a sense of fulfillment and redemption, leaving the audience caught between empathy and profound shock.

2025 Venice Film Festival
2025 Zurich Film Festival

title:地獄新娘（數位修復版）:
Upon hearing that her sister has drowned, Rui-mei, an overseas Chinese woman, returns to Taiwan in secret. Concealing her true identity, she moves into her brother-in-law’s home as a tutor for her niece, determined to uncover the truth behind the tragedy. As they spend their days together, she not only helps mend the fractured bond between father and daughter but also finds herself falling for her brother-in-law. Amidst the shadows of a haunted mansion, the discovery of a secret diary, and a web of tangled emotions, the truth slowly begins to emerge. But just as her brother-in-law proposes, a deadly conspiracy erupts on the eve of their wedding.

Drawing inspiration from the Brontë sisters and Daphne du Maurier, British author Victoria Holt rose to fame in 1960 with her Gothic romance *Mistress of Mellyn*. After its serialization in Taiwanese newspapers and subsequent publication by Crown Press, the novel ignited a craze for Western romance across the island. Director Hsin Chi masterfully transplants the British suspense and romance of the original novel into the local atmosphere of Tamsui. With sophisticated staging and cinematography, the film not only demonstrates high production standards but also proves that Taiwanese filmmakers were capable of far more than just tragic melodramas—showcasing their ability to master international genres and literary adaptations with a diverse creative vision.
title:女孩不平凡:
★ 2025 Official Selection, "Vision" Competition, 30th Busan International Film Festival
★ 2025 Nominee, Best Supporting Actress, 62nd Golden Horse Awards

As time flows, love is sought across three cities.

Hong Kong, age 34: She is a director whose latest project has stalled. Trapped in a creative drought and caught at a crossroads with her girlfriend over the question of "marriage," she finds herself paralyzed by indecision.

Taiwan, age 22: She is a stranger studying abroad alone. Wandering the boundary between freedom and bewilderment, she is locked in a volatile, love-hate relationship, burning with a passion that leaves the future blurred.

Macau, age 17: She is a gentle, obedient girl. But after meeting an older student that summer, the windows to her heart are flung open, igniting a sudden longing for the world—even if the cost remains unknown.
title:影城介紹:
★ Based on the true story of a guide dog's life! Relive the heartwarming animal cinema classic that moved millions.
★ A rare exploration of the guide dog theme, using a delicate narrative to sincerely portray the profound bond between humans and dogs.
★ A nuanced portrayal of the challenges faced by the visually impaired, witnessing the emotional journey from initial resistance to ultimate trust.

Five adorable Labrador puppies are born into a Tokyo household. One stands out from the rest, marked by a unique cross-shaped black birthmark on its belly. Since the puppies' father was a guide dog, their owner harbors a long-held hope that one of them will follow in his footsteps and become an exceptional guide dog. Because the mother was not a guide dog, the owner’s requests are repeatedly rejected. Undeterred, her persistence finally pays off when a trainer from a guide dog school reluctantly agrees to take one of the puppies for a trial. The puppy with the birthmark is the one chosen, marking the very first separation of its life.

title:影武者:
1980 Cannes Film Festival – Palme d'Or
1981 Mainichi Film Awards – Best Film, Director, Actor, Art Direction, and Music
1981 Academy Awards – Nominated for Best Foreign Language Film and Best Art Direction

During the height of Japan’s Sengoku period, the formidable warlord Takeda Shingen dominates the land with his brilliant leadership, only to be unexpectedly shot on the battlefield. On his deathbed, he orders his inner circle to keep his passing a secret to maintain morale and deter his rivals. Desperate to maintain order, his retainers recruit a common criminal who bears a striking resemblance to Shingen to serve as his "Kagemusha" (Shadow Warrior). As the political landscape shifts like the wind, enemies surround them like a forest, and war rages like fire, can this double embody Shingen’s tiger-like majesty and remain as immovable as a mountain while commanding the clan?

Early in production, Akira Kurosawa clashed with the original lead actor, leading Tatsuya Nakadai to step in at the last minute to play both Takeda Shingen and the Shadow Warrior. His nuanced performance, seamlessly shifting between the aura of a legendary general and the coarseness of a commoner, helped Kurosawa secure the Palme d'Or and served as a precursor to their collaboration on *Ran*. This epic production nearly collapsed due to a lack of funding, but was saved when renowned directors George Lucas and Francis Ford Coppola stepped in to help secure international financing—a story that has since become legendary in the film world.
title:惡女花魁:
The directorial debut of Japanese photography icon Mika Ninagawa returns to the big screen. 

Sold to the Tamagiku-ya house in Yoshiwara at the age of eight, young Kiyoha begins her journey in the world of courtesans. Naturally rebellious and fiercely competitive, she flourishes under the mentorship of the high-ranking Shohi, eventually growing into a dazzling beauty who commands every eye in the room. Navigating a decadent world of luxury and vice, Kiyoha defies the traditional submissiveness of her peers, instead using her "cool and aloof" persona as the ultimate weapon to captivate men. As the years pass, she follows the same path as those before her—weathering heartbreak, disillusionment, and wild abandon—until she finally ascends to the rank of Oiran, the supreme courtesan of Yoshiwara.

Yet, just as she reaches the pinnacle of her career, an unforgettable shower of cherry blossoms alters her destiny once more, leading to a rebellious final choice that leaves everyone stunned. 

Based on the manga by Moyoco Anno, with music directed by J-rock queen Sheena Ringo and starring the iconic Anna Tsuchiya, this film is an explosive showcase of female creative power—a true feast for both the eyes and ears.
title:我只是個士兵:
At the outbreak of the Russo-Ukrainian War, the director volunteered to enlist to document the battlefield firsthand, but military life proved far more difficult to adapt to than he had imagined. As the conflict ground into a stalemate, the bloodshed, cruelty, and isolation began to erode his sensibilities as a chronicler. When death claims loved ones and comrades, leaving only the spread of hatred and rendering life as cheap as weeds, is humanity spread too thin to find a place to dwell? Once one becomes a machine that pulls the trigger without hesitation, is it possible to reclaim the once-familiar comforts of love and family?

Filmed over three and a half years on Ukraine’s frontline, the film follows a young filmmaker who trades his camera for a rifle and joins the Territorial Defence Forces. As war reshapes his life, he moves from storyteller to soldier, documenting fear, loss, and resilience amid chaos.

2025 Sheffield Doc/Fest
2025 Toronto IFF

title:日本的悲劇:
Color | 101 min | 2012
IFF Rotterdam 2012, Busan IFF 2012
Special Mention, 2013 Osaka Asian Film Festival

After the sudden passing of his beloved wife and with his dejected only son facing middle-aged unemployment, an ailing old carpenter realizes his days are numbered. He decides to nail his bedroom door shut and starve himself, instructing his son not to break in until he finds a job so they can continue collecting pension payments to survive. What was once a blissful three-generation household is now fractured by death and mired in suffering; their lives have lost all color overnight. Separated by a single door, can the father and son rediscover their bond, regain the will to live, and see their journey through to the end?

At 80 years old, Tatsuya Nakadai remained a formidable force on screen and stage. Here, he delivers a raw, unadorned performance as a grieving father who has lost the will to live. Through a series of intense close-ups, he masterfully captures the character’s profound despair. He is joined by the talented Kazuki Kitamura and Berlin Silver Bear winner Shinobu Terajima, who both deliver standout performances. Following *Haru’s Journey*, which similarly explored themes of aging and mortality, Nakadai and director Masahiro Kobayashi reunite for this adaptation of a notorious true case, casting a spotlight on the harsh realities of Japan’s aging society. Kobayashi tailored the role specifically for Nakadai, approaching the film with the gravity of writing a final testament. The result is a heavy yet authentic exploration of life and death that confronts the psychological trauma of the post-311 era.
title:殺戮戰場的邊緣:
Beginning in 1980, a massive influx of Cambodian and Vietnamese refugees fled the horrors of war to the Thai-Cambodian border, establishing makeshift camps. Five years later, a team from Taiwan’s Kuangchi Program Service traveled to the region, capturing the harrowing reality of refugees risking their lives in minefields to forage for supplies, as well as their resilience as they used song and dance to soothe the spirits of the departed amidst the roar of artillery. As the first Taiwanese documentary to focus on international refugees, it broke the long-standing monopoly of state-owned studios on the Golden Horse Award for Best Documentary and served as a catalyst for the redefinition and critical discussion of the genre in Taiwan.

1986 Golden Horse Awards: Best Documentary, Best Documentary Director
1986 Asia Pacific Film Festival: Best Short Film

Long before her legendary tenure as Éric Rohmer’s long-time editor, Mary Stephen was already a "tailor of images," using her camera to capture the displacement of the diaspora, the struggle for identity, and the growing pains of coming of age. This screening features four of her early works, including her debut feature *Shades of Silk* and three rare shorts of distinct styles—*Labyrinth*, *A Very Easy Death*, and *Pawaganak: The Great Canadian Puberty Rite*. Through the interplay of sound and image, Stephen transforms personal experience into evocative visual poetry. From a Canadian road-trip baptism and the somniloquy of a Parisian student dorm to a quiet gaze at death, her imagery is as delicate and resilient as silk. These works serve as memory fragments drifting between Eastern and Western cultures, documenting her perception and search for selfhood.
title:深度安靜:
★ Nominated for seven awards at the 62nd Golden Horse Awards, including Best New Director, Best Cinematography, Best Art Direction, and Best Leading Actor and Actress.

Yu-ming and Yi-ting first met in the "Deep Quiet Room" of their university library. They fell in love and married, expecting a stable life together. However, after Yi-ting becomes pregnant, past pressures and family shadows gradually resurface, fundamentally altering their relationship. When Yi-ting’s mobility-impaired father moves in, Yu-ming struggles to hold the family together, living through a suffocating existence of polite but distant formalities. Unable to truly understand Yi-ting’s inner world, it is only when Yu-ming reads the book she is currently editing that he realizes the burden she carries is far beyond anything he had ever imagined.
title:畫外流離：革命直至勝利:
Driven by curiosity and a lack of familiarity with Palestinian cinema and history, a film student uses the 1948 "Nakba" (Day of Catastrophe) as a watershed moment, navigating the battles and struggles of the Palestinian people through archival footage. Beginning with classics such as Jean-Luc Godard’s *Notre Musique*, the film pieces together a destiny spanning over 40 years. Composed entirely of Palestinian footage screened at international film festivals prior to 1982, the work directly highlights the techniques and significance of repurposing "displaced images."

A meditation on Palestinian self-representation through the Palestine Film Unit (PFU) of the 1960s–70s. Drawing on globally recovered archival footage, the film traces militant filmmakers reclaiming image and narrative through revolutionary cinema, reflecting on struggles revived on screen—and those that remain off frame.

title:穿越地獄之門:
★ Spain’s Official Entry for Best International Feature Film at the 98th Academy Awards ★
★ Jury Prize, 2025 Cannes Film Festival ★

A father and son travel to the edge of the desert in southern Morocco in search of a long-lost relative. After handing out missing person flyers at a midnight rave, they are led by a group of young revelers deeper into the most desolate reaches of the desert. Swaying between the twilight, the music, and the dunes, they draw closer to the Sirāt—the hair-thin bridge of Islamic legend that spans the void between heaven and hell.

Blending intense electronic beats with sweeping desert landscapes, the film creates an immersive, near-religious experience. The viewing journey is a tapestry of rhythm and wilderness that explores the human condition at the fracture point between nature and civilization.
title:綠的海平線+軍教男兒－台灣軍士教導團的故事:
At the end of World War II, more than 8,000 Taiwanese youths were mobilized by the Japanese government to produce military aircraft at naval factories. In the post-war era, they stood in the fissures between different times and regimes; though their chosen paths varied, they all faced the same grueling struggle to adapt and survive. Facing the weight of history, the film avoids resentment and pathos, opting instead for a warm narration to thread together archival documents and interviews. It gently unfolds the personal strivings and helpless drifts of individuals in a turbulent age, reflecting the complex, overlapping layers of identity and belonging.

2006 Special Jury Award, Taipei Film Awards
2008 Grand Prize, Golden Harvest Awards

**Suspended Duty: Taiwan Military Training Regiment**
KUO Liang-yin｜Taiwan｜2010｜DCP｜Colour｜47 min

During the era of "Resisting Communism and Opposing Russia," over 4,000 young men joined the "Taiwan Military Training Regiment" in 1950, expecting to serve as cadres for a new Taiwanese army upon completing their training. Before long, they were hit with a sudden order of "suspended duty," leaving them on standby for over 50 years without ever being formally discharged. Using a satirical, propaganda-style narration to humorously bridge various interviews, the film reveals how political forces discipline the body, silence dissent, and manipulate thought. Through the firsthand accounts of those who lived through different wars, it invites a deeper contemplation on the question: "For whom do we fight?"

2010 Taiwan International Documentary Festival (TIDF)
2011 Best Documentary, Golden Harvest Awards

title:總統的蛋糕:
★ Iraq’s official entry for the 2026 Academy Award for Best International Feature Film, a dark horse that advanced to the top 15 shortlist.

In 1990 Iraq, war and sanctions have drained the color from the cities, and supplies are so scarce that even sugar has become a luxury. Amidst this hardship, President Saddam Hussein orders the entire nation to celebrate his birthday, decreeing that every school class must present him with a cake or face unimaginable consequences. Nine-year-old Lamia never expected that she would be the one chosen for this daunting task.

Living alone with her grandmother, they can barely afford to bake bread, let alone a grand cake. To save her classmates from punishment, Lamia clutches her beloved rooster and sets off with her grandmother on a journey to the city. What she thought would be a simple trip to gather ingredients turns into a complex and perilous adventure. In the heart of a turbulent city, she witnesses the many faces of adults struggling to survive: the kind and the cunning, those who offer a helping hand, and those who would betray her in a heartbeat.
title:舞林殺手:
Award-winning director Eiji Uchida strikes again with a genre-defying masterpiece that blends high-octane assassin action with the art of dance. A motley crew of professional assassins, each with vastly different ages and temperaments, accepts a high-stakes, big-money contract. Their target is the notorious mob boss Heizo Honjo, but there’s a catch: the only way to get close to him is by winning a dance competition!

With zero experience, no chemistry, and even less motivation, can this ragtag team actually compete in a serious dance tournament? Assembled solely because they supposedly had "prior dance experience," this eccentric squad features: Daiya, an orphanage counselor who is secretly a legendary ex-assassin; Kumashiro, the mission recruiter and second-in-command of the Kazama Syndicate; Kiryu, a cold-blooded lone wolf who hates working in groups; Arata, a hot-headed but fiercely loyal hitman; and Murasame, a washed-up former yakuza. 

Though they initially clash at every turn, these killers soon find themselves seduced by the rhythm, transforming into a formidable force of "dance-floor assassins." As they fight their way through the rounds, will they actually dance their way to the championship?
title:芭比之長髮公主: Long, long ago, in a world filled with dragons and magic, there lived a princess named Rapunzel, who had the longest and most beautiful hair in the land. The witch Gothel kept Rapunzel hidden in a high tower deep within the Forbidden Forest, guarded by a dragon named Hugo and surrounded by an enchanted wall. Day and night, Rapunzel was at the beck and call of Gothel’s servants; her favorite passion, painting, could only be done in secret when no one was watching. By a twist of fate, Rapunzel accidentally discovers an ancient tunnel and, risking Gothel’s wrath, ventures out into the world alone. Adapted from the Brothers Grimm fairy tale "Rapunzel," this animation brings dragons, witches, and towers to life while infusing the story with unexpected magic. Rapunzel, portrayed by Barbie, possesses a magic paintbrush that makes anything she paints come true. With creativity and magic as her weapons, Barbie bravely breaks free from the curse.
title:芭比與胡桃鉗的夢幻之旅:
Barbie stars as Clara, who receives a wooden Nutcracker soldier from her aunt on Christmas Eve. That night, while the rest of the family is asleep, the evil Mouse King and his army invade Clara’s living room. Suddenly, the Nutcracker springs to life to fend off the invaders. The fierce battle wakes Clara, and as she rushes to help, the Mouse King casts an evil spell that shrinks her! To break the curse, Clara and the Nutcracker embark on a spectacular adventure through a fantasy world, searching for the Sugarplum Princess to set everything right.

Blending fairy tales with classical music, this film creates a magical and elegant Barbie world. In Barbie’s first-ever animated movie, adapted from E.T.A. Hoffmann’s classic "The Nutcracker and the Mouse King," Barbie dances through a dreamlike kingdom with the Nutcracker to the iconic music of Tchaikovsky’s famous ballet.
title:蝴蝶夢:
Winner of the 1941 Academy Awards for Best Picture and Best Cinematography (Black-and-White), and selected for preservation in the National Film Registry in 2018. 

A young woman meets a British gentleman in a foreign land, falls head over heels, and marries him in a whirlwind romance. However, upon returning to his grand estate in Cornwall, she discovers the ancient manor is no honeymoon nest, but a labyrinth haunted by the shadow of his late wife. From the furnishings and old clothes to every corner of the house, everything seems to whisper her presence. As the young bride sinks into anxiety, the ice-cold housekeeper constantly reminds her that she can never replace her predecessor. When a shipwreck is salvaged and brought ashore, the lies and mysteries of death hidden deep within the estate begin to surface.

Alfred Hitchcock’s first American film, adapted from Daphne du Maurier’s 1938 novel of the same name, uses high-contrast black-and-white cinematography to recreate a world of suspense and obsession. Despite the strict constraints of the Hays Code, the film subtly touches on taboos such as marital infidelity and lesbian undertones. Du Maurier, a master of Gothic romance, possessed a style so influential that it inspired the novel *Mistress of Milan* (the basis for *Corpse Bride*) and even led some to believe she was its true author.
title:讓建築歌唱～徐亞英的交響人生:
Renowned Chinese-American architectural acoustics master Xu Yaying spent decades collaborating with Pritzker Prize-winning legends such as I.M. Pei and Frank Gehry. Celebrated for his "invisible" acoustic designs, Xu contributed to the design and construction of nearly a hundred cultural and artistic venues worldwide. His late-career magnum opus—and the project he takes the most pride in—is Taiwan’s National Kaohsiung Center for the Arts (Weiwuying).

Architectural acoustics is a synthesis of physics, music, architecture, and science. An acoustician enables music to flow through a meticulously designed space, ensuring that beautiful melodies can truly move the human heart. Following Xu’s lean yet steady footsteps, we revisit his most iconic masterpieces, including the Louvre Pyramid and the Philharmonie Luxembourg. He recounts his journey of solving complex challenges for architects, revealing how he earned the nickname "Mr. Why Not" from Christian de Portzamparc.
title:谷的故事集: Situated on the outskirts of Barcelona, Vallbona is a world apart, hemmed in by highways, railways, and the Besòs River. Originally settled by post-war coal workers, the neighborhood has since welcomed waves of immigrants, evolving into a unique global village. Captured in black and white, the film documents the passage of time through the candid conversations of its residents. Antonio, the
title:路有多長: In the coastal community of 'Etolan, where the mountains meet the sea, a second-generation Mainlander director meets Amis artist Siki Sufin. Through their encounter, he learns of a "lost generation" of Indigenous youth who were taken to China during the Chinese Civil War, disappearing from their homeland forever. Bound by a shared understanding of their fathers' nostalgia, their distinct life experiences resonate as they embark on a journey together to seek out the surviving veterans of the tribe. Through the lens, individual faces interweave into a collective memory of the era, while the helplessness and confusion of identities shifted by political tides are laid bare in their reminiscing gazes.
title:鍵:
1959 Kinema Junpo Top 10 Japanese Films (9th Place)
1960 Golden Globe Award for Best Foreign Language Film
1960 Cannes Film Festival Jury Prize

Antique appraiser Kenmochi, in a desperate bid to defy aging, devises a dangerous scheme: he maneuvers Kimura, his daughter’s physician fiancé, into an affair with his wife, Ikuko, hoping that jealousy will reignite his waning virility. As the voyeuristic perspectives shift, the prim wife’s awakening desires, the future son-in-law’s restless ambition, and the daughter’s cold detachment all come to light. In this web of calculated lust, what fate awaits them?

Marking his second consecutive collaboration with director Kon Ichikawa, Tatsuya Nakadai delivers a chillingly intellectual performance as Kimura. This sinister turn stands in stark contrast to his moralistic role in *The Human Condition*, cementing his reputation for versatility between hero and villain. Adapted from Jun'ichiro Tanizaki’s novel, Ichikawa and screenwriters Natto Wada and Keiji Hasebe transform the original’s dark, aesthetic eroticism into a biting black comedy. Captured through Kazuo Miyagawa’s highly stylized cinematography, the film uses light and shadow to maintain a clinical, detached gaze on the characters' spiraling desires.
title:鏡之第三樂章: ★ A Cahiers du Cinéma Top 10 Film of 2025. While on a weekend getaway, piano student Laura is involved in a car accident. Though she emerges physically unscathed, her boyfriend is killed, leaving her consumed by shock and a profound sense of emptiness. In the German countryside, she encounters a woman who witnessed the crash. Struck by Laura’s resemblance to her own late daughter, the woman kindly takes her into her idyllic family home. However, this seemingly warm sanctuary is fraught with unspoken projections and substitutions. As the woman attempts to fill the void of her loss with Laura, the young student begins to feel suffocated by the weight of the care and expectations thrust upon her.
title:門對門移動書店:
Based on the heartwarming bestseller that topped the charts for 100 weeks, *The Door-to-Door Bookstore* is a box office sensation that earned over NT$200 million in Germany.

Carl, a dedicated "book walker," lives a life as precise as clockwork. Every day, he follows the exact same path, hand-delivering carefully wrapped books to the doorsteps of his loyal customers. He knows the tastes of everyone in town and takes great pride in his ability to select the perfect book for each person.

One day, Carl’s orderly world is unexpectedly interrupted. Nine-year-old Schascha, who is still healing from the loss of her mother, decides to join Carl on his rounds. Not only does she find solace in the books and the journey, but her unique perspective on life also helps Carl open his heart, sparking an unexpected friendship.

However, a sudden turn of events causes Carl to lose his job. The once-vibrant town, once filled with the fragrance of books, begins to fall into a lonely silence. As the community drifts apart, Schascha decides to rely on the power she believes in most: the magic of a good book to bring people together. Joining forces with Carl, she sets out to create a profound and positive change—not just for their readers, but for themselves as well.
title:隱蹟之書：重寫自我:
Toronto IFF 2025
IDFA 2025
Golden Horse Awards: Best Documentary Feature & Best Editing

Veteran editor Mary Stephen possesses a distinctly East Asian face yet bears a Western surname: Stephen. Delving into her family’s little-known history through her father’s journals, she discovers that both her parents lived under assumed names with pasts where truth and fiction blur. Her father appears to be a descendant of Australian Aboriginal fishermen, while her mother—a woman who once harbored literary dreams—seems to have had a subtle connection to a romance in China involving a member of Virginia Woolf’s family.

In her quest to uncover the origins of the "Stephen" name, she sifts through home movies and old photographs, reconstructing a private family history that feels truer than reality itself amidst an era of colonial expansion and upheaval. Navigating a maze of multiple narratives, she interrogates her own identity, exploring how individuals can find flight once more through the fog of diaspora by rewriting their own history. Together, she pieces together a profound cinematic portrait of life spanning borders and a century of global change.
title_norm:compact disc 記憶座標:
When an entire generation is forced to forget rather than choosing to, how can memory be preserved? Can friendship become a gentle yet persistent form of resistance? The camera follows the director and a close friend as they return to the black hole of memory; through low-key and restrained imagery, unspeakable emotions are left to ferment in the dark. More than just a documentary, this is a memorandum of youth written for one another—an exercise in collective remembering. Amidst gloomy times, through love and companionship, it pieces together a bewildered yet authentic portrait of a generation of Hong Kongers.

**2026 CPH:DOX Copenhagen International Documentary Film Festival 【Asian Premiere】**

**Map of Traces**
CHAN Hau-chun｜Hong Kong｜2025｜DCP｜Colour, B&W｜29 min

A reflection on the landscapes of Hong Kong and the subtle traces left behind by its people. Within the memories of those who have left, stayed, or drifted, the film searches for intimate moments to connect one another. Blending media memories of Hong Kong’s major thoroughfares, correspondence with friends in prison, and street art, it explores the precise yet fluid moments between "presence" and "absence."

Unfolding as a tender letter, the film traces Hong Kong through its landscapes and lingering marks. Within memories of those who left, stayed, or drifted, it searches for intimate moments that quietly connect lives, attuned to the city’s subtle rhythms of change and stillness.

title_norm:jsa共同警戒區 4k數位修復版:
The masterpiece that established director Park Chan-wook’s international reputation and became the highest-grossing South Korean film of its year, *Joint Security Area* returns for a limited 26th-anniversary 4K restored theatrical release. Through a cinematic lens that interweaves suspense and human nature, the film exposes both cruel realities and heartwarming humanity.

In the Joint Security Area (JSA) on the border between North and South Korea, two North Korean soldiers are shot dead one night under mysterious circumstances. Sophie, a Swiss-Korean female officer from a neutral nation, is dispatched to investigate the baffling case. As she peels back the layers of the investigation, she uncovers a shocking truth tied to a secret friendship that transcends national borders and historical animosity. Even as nations are pitted against each other by political hatred, a bond of brotherhood manages to cross the invisible 38th parallel. Through a seemingly casual yet precious photograph, the film conveys a profound yearning for peaceful coexistence.
title_norm:videotage x forum lenteng 鱗光映:
"Knock, knock, knock." A knock at the door in the dead of night shatters the silence. At first, only the woman hears it; a few days later, the same sound returns, and this time, her husband hears it too. Who is outside? Who is disturbing them from the shadows? Through interviews with a couple who have just moved into a new home, and using everyday objects like chalk and photographs, this thriller experience is reconstructed frame by frame. Private anxieties seep into the Indonesian cultural context, weaving a lingering atmosphere of horror.

2003 SGIFF Singapore International Film Festival

title_norm:世外:
★ 2025 Golden Horse Award for Best Animated Feature

After death and before reincarnation, departed souls arrive at a mystical realm known as "The Beyond." Here, Spirit Guardians spend their days guiding souls toward their next lives. This cycle continues until a young Spirit Guardian, Little Ghost, encounters "Little Sister," a soul who refuses to move on. Through her, the once-emotionless Little Ghost begins to see The Beyond in a completely different light.

As he attempts to lead her toward rebirth, Little Ghost uncovers the regrets of her past life. Overwhelmed by fury, Little Sister triggers an irreversible curse. If she is completely consumed by her hatred, both The Beyond and the mortal realm will be reduced to ash along with her.

Little Ghost convinces the Celestial Maiden to grant Little Sister a final chance at redemption. The Maiden dispatches the formidable warrior Heitian to accompany them on a perilous quest spanning a thousand years. Despite the constant dangers, Little Ghost repeatedly risks his own life to save her, hoping only that Little Sister can let go of her past pain, forgive herself, and finally step lightly onto the path of reincarnation.
title_norm:亂:
1986 Blue Ribbon Awards: Best Film
1986 Mainichi Film Awards: Best Film, Best Director, Best Supporting Actor
1986 Academy Awards: Winner for Best Costume Design; Nominated for Best Director, Best Cinematography, and Best Art Direction

Lord Hidetora Ichimonji, a Sengoku-era warlord who once dominated the battlefield with peerless authority, finds himself weary and aging at seventy. He decides to abdicate and divide his castles among his three sons. However, his eldest son immediately turns against him, subjecting him to humiliation and forcing him into exile. His second son also plots betrayal, treating his father with cold indifference. Only the third son—who was banished for his blunt honesty—secretly sends protection and warmly welcomes his exiled father. Witnessing the bloody fratricide of his lineage and tormented by the sins of his past, the once-mighty Hidetora descends into madness, silenced by the cruel tides of fate.

A thematic counterpart to *Kagemusha* that reaches even greater artistic heights, the film masterfully weaves Shakespeare’s tragedy *King Lear* into the Japanese Warring States period. Tatsuya Nakadai’s profound acting creates an intense emotional resonance, serving as the cornerstone of the film’s grand epic spirit. Although the production reused costumes and props from *Kagemusha*, it still cost $10 million to stage its massive battle sequences—a record-breaking budget in Japanese cinema at the time. The result is Akira Kurosawa’s ultimate masterpiece, the culmination of a lifetime of cinematic mastery.
title_norm:二百三高地:
1981 Blue Ribbon Awards: Best Actor & Best Supporting Actor
1981 Kinema Junpo: Readers' Choice Award
1981 Japan Academy Film Prize: Winner of Best Actor, Cinematography, and Lighting; Nominated for Best Picture, Director, Screenplay, Best Supporting Actress, Art Direction, Score, and Sound.

The Meiji Restoration propelled Japan into the ranks of the world's leading powers. Its expansionist ambitions toward Northeast China and Korea provoked the Russian Empire, a traditional European power, bringing the two nations to the brink of war. To secure control over Port Arthur, the Russian army constructed what was considered the most formidable fortress in military history on the nearby "203 Hill," prepared to deliver a crushing blow to the Japanese. General Maresuke Nogi, fiercely loyal to the Emperor, led the well-equipped Third Army, determined to seize this strategic stronghold at any cost.

In a departure from his role in *Kagemusha* released that same year, Tatsuya Nakadai portrays the "War God" Nogi with restrained, commanding authority. His nuanced performance captures the crushing emotional toll of duty, a legendary portrayal that moved audiences to tears. Toei spared no expense in creating magnificent sets, enlisting Toshio Masuda—renowned for his war epics—to direct. The film’s contrast between brutal, bloody combat and the pure essence of the Bushido spirit sparked intense debate and ignited a trend for large-scale war spectacles. Following the normalization of diplomatic relations between Japan and China in 1972, Japanese films were banned from import in Taiwan; this film was screened only once during the inaugural Golden Horse International Film Exhibition in 1980 and has not been released there since.
title_norm:人生海海:
★ Winner of the Best Supporting Actress at the 62nd Golden Horse Awards.

After years of working in Taiwan, Ah Yao returns to his hometown to handle his father’s funeral alongside his younger siblings. However, the proceedings are disrupted when the Religious Affairs Bureau intervenes, attempting to seize the father’s remains. In the face of this sudden conflict, the three siblings react in starkly different ways: the eldest brother, Ah Yao, chooses to yield; the younger brother, Ah Cai, opts for direct confrontation; and their sister, Shui Yun, struggles to maintain the sanctity of the ritual and order.

As the funeral progresses, long-buried family secrets begin to surface—tales of smuggling, migration, illness, and the lifelong struggle to obtain legal identification. Caught between memory and reality, the three are forced to re-examine their father and confront their own sense of belonging in this land.
title_norm:人間的條件 第一部 純愛篇・第二部 激怒篇:
Top 10 Japanese Films (5th Place) & Best Actress, Kinema Junpo 1960
San Giorgio Prize & Premio Pasinetti, Venice Film Festival 1960

In 1943, Kaji, a man driven by humanist ideals, travels with his wife Michiko to the Tiger Ridge mine in Manchuria to serve as a wartime labor supervisor. He attempts to reform the colonial system from within and improve the treatment of prisoners of war, but he is constantly marginalized by bureaucrats and pressured by the military, all while failing to win the trust of the mistreated Chinese laborers. As the machinery of war relentlessly strips away his choices, Kaji finds himself increasingly isolated. How can he protect the dignity of those around him? And how can he preserve his own conscience as a human being?

Following *Black River*, a young Tatsuya Nakadai collaborates with director Masaki Kobayashi for the second time. His righteous and restrained performance captures Kaji’s impossible dilemma, marking a major breakthrough in his acting career. This realist masterpiece, adapted from Junpei Gomikawa’s novel of the same name, stands as a pinnacle of Japanese anti-war cinema. Cinematographer Yoshio Miyajima’s static shots and black-and-white widescreen photography establish a steady, solemn visual style, grounding this cinematic epic in a cold and piercing gaze at reality.
title_norm:仲代達矢，為演員而活:
In 1975, Tatsuya Nakadai and his creative partner and wife, Kyoko Miyazaki, co-founded the acting school Mumeijuku. Nakadai personally oversees talent selection and intensive training, maintaining a rigorous schedule from 6 a.m. to 8 p.m. When performing, he suppresses his own presence, allowing only the character to collide with his scene partner. Mumeijuku does more than just train actors; it cultivates "human beings" capable of standing on their own in the world, producing a steady stream of talent for 50 years. Yet, beneath his roles as a teacher and a performer, Nakadai’s true self is that of a humble actor who hand-copies his lines with a calligraphy brush and pastes them on his bedroom walls for constant memorization. 

Director Hidetaka Inozuka spent over a year following Nakadai, capturing the training process at Mumeijuku and its theatrical performances. Interspersed with interviews with Nakadai and his disciples, the film documents a side of him rarely seen by the public. At the time of filming, Nakadai was in his 80s, yet he remained steadfast on his beloved stage, battling a fading memory and declining physical strength. He continues to embrace the "beginner’s mind" of his early days as a "nobody," fulfilling his life’s mission—to live for the craft of acting—and passing this conviction on to the next generation.
title_norm:切腹:
1962 Kinema Junpo Top 10 Japanese Films (3rd Place)
1963 Cannes Film Festival Special Jury Prize

In 1630 (the seventh year of the Kanei era), the centralized rule of the third Tokugawa Shogun, Iemitsu, weakened the power of the daimyo houses, leaving once-proud samurai to wander as masterless ronin. During this time, many ronin began extorting money from daimyo estates by threatening to commit ritual suicide (seppuku) on their grounds—a nuisance that plagued many, including the prestigious Ii clan. One day, an elderly ronin named Tsugumo Hanshiro arrives at the Ii estate, requesting a place to commit seppuku. The clan's retainers decide to call his bluff, intending to force him to follow through with the act. However, as the old man slowly recounts the story of his journey, his true intentions are gradually revealed.

Following *The Human Condition*, Tatsuya Nakadai and Masaki Kobayashi collaborate once again. Based on Yasuhiko Takiguchi's novel *Ibun Ronin-ki* and written by legendary screenwriter Shinobu Hashimoto, the film employs a non-linear narrative to build a masterful atmosphere of suspense. Captured by cinematographer Yoshio Miyajima, known for his grand visual scale, the film depicts a tragic and brutal tale of revenge. In an era and nation that glorified the code of Bushido, this film offers a bold and scathing critique—a spirit that Tatsuya Nakadai has frequently praised, citing this as one of his favorite works.
title_norm:刺蠻王:
The swordsman Jin Shange originally intended to serve his country by joining the ranks of Marshal Zhang Zhe. However, he was framed for the murder of the Prime Minister's son and sentenced to exile at the frontier. During his transport, the escort was ambushed by Jin soldiers; in the chaos, Jin Shange broke free from his shackles, defeated the enemy, and escaped.

Meanwhile, Marshal Zhang Zhe was killed by the Jin commander, Wanyan. The Marshal's daughter, Enzhu, managed to flee to Lu Village with the help of Wang Feng. Seeking vengeance, they began recruiting heroes to their cause. Jin Shange happened to pass by their martial arts tournament, where his peerless skills impressed Wang Feng, who invited him to join the resistance against the Jin invaders. Jin Shange readily agreed, but on one condition: he must marry Enzhu. To avenge her father, Enzhu consented, provided that Jin Shange first return with Wanyan’s head.

Jin Shange initially presents himself as a decadent, roguish, and even lecherous figure, subverting the traditional "chivalrous hero" archetype of wuxia cinema. As the group unites to fight the Jin, this unconventional warrior gradually redeems his image, ultimately making a stylish, solitary exit reminiscent of a classic Western hero.
title_norm:喀布爾，信仰之間:
Amidst the echoes of prayer and rifles slung over shoulders, faith and weaponry define the everyday. Two devout Afghan brothers live within this world: the elder patrols his post, dreaming of martyrdom while burdened by mundane worries; the younger, still a child, spends his days herding, playing, and reciting scripture, looking up to his brother with an innocence written across his face. Eschewing judgment for an open lens, the film offers a microscopic look at the coming-of-age of males under Taliban theocracy. What outsiders perceive as indoctrination is, to them, a sense of fulfillment and redemption, leaving the audience caught between empathy and profound shock.

title_norm:地獄新娘 數位修復版:
Upon hearing that her sister has drowned, Rui-mei, an overseas Chinese woman, returns to Taiwan in secret. Concealing her true identity, she moves into her brother-in-law’s home as a tutor for her niece, determined to uncover the truth behind the tragedy. As they spend their days together, she not only helps mend the fractured bond between father and daughter but also finds herself falling for her brother-in-law. Amidst the shadows of a haunted mansion, the discovery of a secret diary, and a web of tangled emotions, the truth slowly begins to emerge. But just as her brother-in-law proposes, a deadly conspiracy erupts on the eve of their wedding.

Drawing inspiration from the Brontë sisters and Daphne du Maurier, British author Victoria Holt rose to fame in 1960 with her Gothic romance *Mistress of Mellyn*. After its serialization in Taiwanese newspapers and subsequent publication by Crown Press, the novel ignited a craze for Western romance across the island. Director Hsin Chi masterfully transplants the British suspense and romance of the original novel into the local atmosphere of Tamsui. With sophisticated staging and cinematography, the film not only demonstrates high production standards but also proves that Taiwanese filmmakers were capable of far more than just tragic melodramas—showcasing their ability to master international genres and literary adaptations with a diverse creative vision.
title_norm:女孩不平凡:
★ 2025 Official Selection, "Vision" Competition, 30th Busan International Film Festival
★ 2025 Nominee, Best Supporting Actress, 62nd Golden Horse Awards

As time flows, love is sought across three cities.

Hong Kong, age 34: She is a director whose latest project has stalled. Trapped in a creative drought and caught at a crossroads with her girlfriend over the question of "marriage," she finds herself paralyzed by indecision.

Taiwan, age 22: She is a stranger studying abroad alone. Wandering the boundary between freedom and bewilderment, she is locked in a volatile, love-hate relationship, burning with a passion that leaves the future blurred.

Macau, age 17: She is a gentle, obedient girl. But after meeting an older student that summer, the windows to her heart are flung open, igniting a sudden longing for the world—even if the cost remains unknown.
title_norm:影城介紹:
★ Based on the true story of a guide dog's life! Relive the heartwarming animal cinema classic that moved millions.
★ A rare exploration of the guide dog theme, using a delicate narrative to sincerely portray the profound bond between humans and dogs.
★ A nuanced portrayal of the challenges faced by the visually impaired, witnessing the emotional journey from initial resistance to ultimate trust.

Five adorable Labrador puppies are born into a Tokyo household. One stands out from the rest, marked by a unique cross-shaped black birthmark on its belly. Since the puppies' father was a guide dog, their owner harbors a long-held hope that one of them will follow in his footsteps and become an exceptional guide dog. Because the mother was not a guide dog, the owner’s requests are repeatedly rejected. Undeterred, her persistence finally pays off when a trainer from a guide dog school reluctantly agrees to take one of the puppies for a trial. The puppy with the birthmark is the one chosen, marking the very first separation of its life.

During the height of Japan’s Sengoku period, the formidable warlord Takeda Shingen dominates the land with his brilliant leadership, only to be unexpectedly shot on the battlefield. On his deathbed, he orders his inner circle to keep his passing a secret to maintain morale and deter his rivals. Desperate to maintain order, his retainers recruit a common criminal who bears a striking resemblance to Shingen to serve as his "Kagemusha" (Shadow Warrior). As the political landscape shifts like the wind, enemies surround them like a forest, and war rages like fire, can this double embody Shingen’s tiger-like majesty and remain as immovable as a mountain while commanding the clan?

Early in production, Akira Kurosawa clashed with the original lead actor, leading Tatsuya Nakadai to step in at the last minute to play both Takeda Shingen and the Shadow Warrior. His nuanced performance, seamlessly shifting between the aura of a legendary general and the coarseness of a commoner, helped Kurosawa secure the Palme d'Or and served as a precursor to their collaboration on *Ran*. This epic production nearly collapsed due to a lack of funding, but was saved when renowned directors George Lucas and Francis Ford Coppola stepped in to help secure international financing—a story that has since become legendary in the film world.
title_norm:惡女花魁:
The directorial debut of Japanese photography icon Mika Ninagawa returns to the big screen. 

Sold to the Tamagiku-ya house in Yoshiwara at the age of eight, young Kiyoha begins her journey in the world of courtesans. Naturally rebellious and fiercely competitive, she flourishes under the mentorship of the high-ranking Shohi, eventually growing into a dazzling beauty who commands every eye in the room. Navigating a decadent world of luxury and vice, Kiyoha defies the traditional submissiveness of her peers, instead using her "cool and aloof" persona as the ultimate weapon to captivate men. As the years pass, she follows the same path as those before her—weathering heartbreak, disillusionment, and wild abandon—until she finally ascends to the rank of Oiran, the supreme courtesan of Yoshiwara.

Yet, just as she reaches the pinnacle of her career, an unforgettable shower of cherry blossoms alters her destiny once more, leading to a rebellious final choice that leaves everyone stunned. 

Based on the manga by Moyoco Anno, with music directed by J-rock queen Sheena Ringo and starring the iconic Anna Tsuchiya, this film is an explosive showcase of female creative power—a true feast for both the eyes and ears.
title_norm:我只是個士兵:
At the outbreak of the Russo-Ukrainian War, the director volunteered to enlist to document the battlefield firsthand, but military life proved far more difficult to adapt to than he had imagined. As the conflict ground into a stalemate, the bloodshed, cruelty, and isolation began to erode his sensibilities as a chronicler. When death claims loved ones and comrades, leaving only the spread of hatred and rendering life as cheap as weeds, is humanity spread too thin to find a place to dwell? Once one becomes a machine that pulls the trigger without hesitation, is it possible to reclaim the once-familiar comforts of love and family?

Filmed over three and a half years on Ukraine’s frontline, the film follows a young filmmaker who trades his camera for a rifle and joins the Territorial Defence Forces. As war reshapes his life, he moves from storyteller to soldier, documenting fear, loss, and resilience amid chaos.

2025 Sheffield Doc/Fest
2025 Toronto IFF

title_norm:日本的悲劇:
Color | 101 min | 2012
IFF Rotterdam 2012, Busan IFF 2012
Special Mention, 2013 Osaka Asian Film Festival

After the sudden passing of his beloved wife and with his dejected only son facing middle-aged unemployment, an ailing old carpenter realizes his days are numbered. He decides to nail his bedroom door shut and starve himself, instructing his son not to break in until he finds a job so they can continue collecting pension payments to survive. What was once a blissful three-generation household is now fractured by death and mired in suffering; their lives have lost all color overnight. Separated by a single door, can the father and son rediscover their bond, regain the will to live, and see their journey through to the end?

At 80 years old, Tatsuya Nakadai remained a formidable force on screen and stage. Here, he delivers a raw, unadorned performance as a grieving father who has lost the will to live. Through a series of intense close-ups, he masterfully captures the character’s profound despair. He is joined by the talented Kazuki Kitamura and Berlin Silver Bear winner Shinobu Terajima, who both deliver standout performances. Following *Haru’s Journey*, which similarly explored themes of aging and mortality, Nakadai and director Masahiro Kobayashi reunite for this adaptation of a notorious true case, casting a spotlight on the harsh realities of Japan’s aging society. Kobayashi tailored the role specifically for Nakadai, approaching the film with the gravity of writing a final testament. The result is a heavy yet authentic exploration of life and death that confronts the psychological trauma of the post-311 era.
title_norm:殺戮戰場的邊緣:
Beginning in 1980, a massive influx of Cambodian and Vietnamese refugees fled the horrors of war to the Thai-Cambodian border, establishing makeshift camps. Five years later, a team from Taiwan’s Kuangchi Program Service traveled to the region, capturing the harrowing reality of refugees risking their lives in minefields to forage for supplies, as well as their resilience as they used song and dance to soothe the spirits of the departed amidst the roar of artillery. As the first Taiwanese documentary to focus on international refugees, it broke the long-standing monopoly of state-owned studios on the Golden Horse Award for Best Documentary and served as a catalyst for the redefinition and critical discussion of the genre in Taiwan.

1986 Golden Horse Awards: Best Documentary, Best Documentary Director
1986 Asia Pacific Film Festival: Best Short Film

title_norm:流光剪影 雪美蓮的影像記憶:
**A Very Easy Death**
Mary STEPHEN｜Canada｜1975｜DCP｜B&W, Color｜9min

**Pawaganak: The Great Canadian Puberty Rite**
Mary STEPHEN｜Canada｜1974｜DCP｜B&W, Color｜19min

**Shades of Silk**
Mary STEPHEN｜France, Canada｜1978｜DCP｜Color｜62min

2025 Toronto IFF
2025 New York FF
2026 IFF Rotterdam

Long before her legendary tenure as Éric Rohmer’s long-time editor, Mary Stephen was already a "tailor of images," using her camera to capture the displacement of the diaspora, the struggle for identity, and the growing pains of coming of age. This screening features four of her early works, including her debut feature *Shades of Silk* and three rare shorts of distinct styles—*Labyrinth*, *A Very Easy Death*, and *Pawaganak: The Great Canadian Puberty Rite*. Through the interplay of sound and image, Stephen transforms personal experience into evocative visual poetry. From a Canadian road-trip baptism and the somniloquy of a Parisian student dorm to a quiet gaze at death, her imagery is as delicate and resilient as silk. These works serve as memory fragments drifting between Eastern and Western cultures, documenting her perception and search for selfhood.
title_norm:深度安靜:
★ Nominated for seven awards at the 62nd Golden Horse Awards, including Best New Director, Best Cinematography, Best Art Direction, and Best Leading Actor and Actress.

Yu-ming and Yi-ting first met in the "Deep Quiet Room" of their university library. They fell in love and married, expecting a stable life together. However, after Yi-ting becomes pregnant, past pressures and family shadows gradually resurface, fundamentally altering their relationship. When Yi-ting’s mobility-impaired father moves in, Yu-ming struggles to hold the family together, living through a suffocating existence of polite but distant formalities. Unable to truly understand Yi-ting’s inner world, it is only when Yu-ming reads the book she is currently editing that he realizes the burden she carries is far beyond anything he had ever imagined.
title_norm:畫外流離 革命直至勝利:
Driven by curiosity and a lack of familiarity with Palestinian cinema and history, a film student uses the 1948 "Nakba" (Day of Catastrophe) as a watershed moment, navigating the battles and struggles of the Palestinian people through archival footage. Beginning with classics such as Jean-Luc Godard’s *Notre Musique*, the film pieces together a destiny spanning over 40 years. Composed entirely of Palestinian footage screened at international film festivals prior to 1982, the work directly highlights the techniques and significance of repurposing "displaced images."

A meditation on Palestinian self-representation through the Palestine Film Unit (PFU) of the 1960s–70s. Drawing on globally recovered archival footage, the film traces militant filmmakers reclaiming image and narrative through revolutionary cinema, reflecting on struggles revived on screen—and those that remain off frame.

2016 Toronto International Film Festival
2017 Berlinale

title_norm:穿越地獄之門:
★ Spain’s Official Entry for Best International Feature Film at the 98th Academy Awards ★
★ Jury Prize, 2025 Cannes Film Festival ★

A father and son travel to the edge of the desert in southern Morocco in search of a long-lost relative. After handing out missing person flyers at a midnight rave, they are led by a group of young revelers deeper into the most desolate reaches of the desert. Swaying between the twilight, the music, and the dunes, they draw closer to the Sirāt—the hair-thin bridge of Islamic legend that spans the void between heaven and hell.

Blending intense electronic beats with sweeping desert landscapes, the film creates an immersive, near-religious experience. The viewing journey is a tapestry of rhythm and wilderness that explores the human condition at the fracture point between nature and civilization.
title_norm:綠的海平線 軍教男兒－台灣軍士教導團的故事:
At the end of World War II, more than 8,000 Taiwanese youths were mobilized by the Japanese government to produce military aircraft at naval factories. In the post-war era, they stood in the fissures between different times and regimes; though their chosen paths varied, they all faced the same grueling struggle to adapt and survive. Facing the weight of history, the film avoids resentment and pathos, opting instead for a warm narration to thread together archival documents and interviews. It gently unfolds the personal strivings and helpless drifts of individuals in a turbulent age, reflecting the complex, overlapping layers of identity and belonging.

2006 Special Jury Award, Taipei Film Awards
2008 Grand Prize, Golden Harvest Awards

**Suspended Duty: Taiwan Military Training Regiment**
KUO Liang-yin｜Taiwan｜2010｜DCP｜Colour｜47 min

During the era of "Resisting Communism and Opposing Russia," over 4,000 young men joined the "Taiwan Military Training Regiment" in 1950, expecting to serve as cadres for a new Taiwanese army upon completing their training. Before long, they were hit with a sudden order of "suspended duty," leaving them on standby for over 50 years without ever being formally discharged. Using a satirical, propaganda-style narration to humorously bridge various interviews, the film reveals how political forces discipline the body, silence dissent, and manipulate thought. Through the firsthand accounts of those who lived through different wars, it invites a deeper contemplation on the question: "For whom do we fight?"

2010 Taiwan International Documentary Festival (TIDF)
2011 Best Documentary, Golden Harvest Awards
